coredata.dc:title: B, N co-doped carbon dots as efficient nanozymes for colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode detection of cholesterol
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Yuyan
Zhang, Ruilin
Hu, Zhiyuan
Wu, Fengshou
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the B, N co-doped carbon dots (B, N-CDs) were synthesized via facile hydrothermal approach with 6-aminopyridine boronic acid as precursor. In addition to emitting intense blue luminescence when exposed to ultraviolet light, the prepared B, N-CDs displayed remarkable peroxidase-like activity, which could efficiently catalyze the oxidation of 3, 3′, 5, 5′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue ox-TMB in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of B, N-CDs increased gradually upon the addition of H2O2. Since cholesterol oxidase (ChOx) can catalyze the oxidation of cholesterol to form H2O2, the as-prepared B, N-CDs was then used as both colorimetric and fluorometric sensors for the detection of cholesterol with detection limit of 0.87 and 2.31 μM, respectively. Finally, the dual-mode approach based on B, N-CDs was effectively utilized for detecting cholesterol levels in serum samples, proving the potential application of B, N-CDs in the field of biological assay.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Peroxidase-like activity
H2O2
Cholesterol
Dual-mode detection

Detection of cholesterol in actual samples
Steady-state kinetic assays of B, N-CDs
Mechanism of cholesterol detection by B, N-CDs
Detection of cholesterol in real samples
Exploration of the cytotoxicity of B, N-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM micrograph; (B) Diameter distribution; (C) XRD pattern and (D) FT-IR spectra of B, N-CDs.
(A) XPS survey full scan of B, N-CDs; High-resolution scan of (B) C 1s; (C) N 1s; (D) O 1s; (E) Fe 2p and (F) B 1s of B, N-CDs.

(A) UV-vis and PL spectra of B, N-CDs; (B) PL spectrum of B, N-CDs under different excitations (260–320 nm); Linear relationships between absorbance and integrated fluorescence intensity of (C) Quinine sulfate, and (D) B, N-CDs.

(A) UV-vis spectrum of the B, N-CDs + TMB + ChOx system with various concentrations of cholesterol; (B) Dynamic range for colorimetric detection of cholesterol; (C) The relationship between cholesterol levels and absorbance; (D) Absorbance of different interfering materials.

(A) PL spectrum of the B, N-CDs + ChOx system after adding different concentrations of cholesterol; (B) Relationship between F/F0 and cholesterol concentration; (C) The linear correlation between F/F0 and cholesterol concentration; (D) F/F0 of different interfering materials.

(A) Mechanism of cholesterol detection by B, N-CDs; (B) Effects of different scavengers on the catalytic activity of B, N-CDs + TMB + H2O2 systems; (C) Effects of different scavengers on the (F–F0/F0) of B, N-CDs + H2O2 system; (D) EPR spectra of B, N-CDs.
Synthetic routes of the B, N-CDs.
Comparison with reported methods for cholesterol detection.
Determination of cholesterol in real sample.

head.ce:title: B, N co-doped carbon dots as efficient nanozymes for colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode detection of cholesterol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Novel Reactor and Green Chemical Technology, Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry, School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan, 430072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Novel Reactor and Green Chemical Technology
Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry
School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Key Laboratory of Novel Reactor and Green Chemical Technology, Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry, School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan, 430072, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Center for Nanoscience and Technology, Beijing, 100190, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Center for Nanoscience and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Center for Nanoscience and Technology, Beijing, 100190, P. R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yuyan Zhu and Ruilin Zhang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the B, N co-doped carbon dots (B, N-CDs) were synthesized via facile hydrothermal approach with 6-aminopyridine boronic acid as precursor. In addition to emitting intense blue luminescence when exposed to ultraviolet light, the prepared B, N-CDs displayed remarkable peroxidase-like activity, which could efficiently catalyze the oxidation of 3, 3′, 5, 5′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue ox-TMB in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of B, N-CDs increased gradually upon the addition of H2O2. Since cholesterol oxidase (ChOx) can catalyze the oxidation of cholesterol to form H2O2, the as-prepared B, N-CDs was then used as both colorimetric and fluorometric sensors for the detection of cholesterol with detection limit of 0.87 and 2.31 μM, respectively. Finally, the dual-mode approach based on B, N-CDs was effectively utilized for detecting cholesterol levels in serum samples, proving the potential application of B, N-CDs in the field of biological assay.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cholesterol, as an indispensable lipid for humans, plays a significant role in organizing the biophysical properties of mammalian cell membranes [1]. Moreover, cholesterol serves as a significant biomarker for numerous illnesses [2], and the high level of cholesterol have been linked to several cardiovascular ailments such as hypertension, inflammatory heart disease, coronary heart disease and atherosclerosis [3]. Meanwhile, the low level of cholesterol may also cause several diseases such as low lipoprotein, hypocholesterolemia, mal-nutrition and sepsis [4]. Thus, it is important to regularly determine the levels of cholesterol in blood serum. So far, various analytical methods for cholesterol detection had been developed, including electrochemical [5], high-performance liquids chromatography [6], colorimetric [7], and fluorometric [8] methods. However, most methods are costly and time consuming, and require complex instrumentation and laboratory set-up [9]. Therefore, there remains a significant demand to establish an effective sensing platform for cholesterol detection.

Carbon dots (CDs) is a new kind of luminescent nanomaterial [10], which has garnered widespread attention due to their excellent properties, including simple preparation, low toxicity, good biocompatibility and tunable photoluminescence (PL) [11–13]. Therefore, they have shown promise in drug delivery [14], bioimaging [15] and sensing [16], particularly for detecting tumor markers [17], small molecules [18] and metal ions [19]. Recently, the CDs-based nanozymes have garnered great attention due to their good biocompatibility and facile preparation. Lin et al. synthesized CA-CDs with excellent cysteine oxidase-mimicking activity using citric acid as carbon source, which could be acted a sensitive fluorescent sensor for cysteine detection [20]. Zhao et al. prepared Mo, S co-doped CQDs with robust peroxidase-like activity, which showed high selectivity for cholesterol detection through colorimetric method [21]. Furthermore, the heteroatom doping may alter the internal electronic environment of CDs, thereby changing their properties and expanding their range of applications [22]. In particular, the doping of metals into CDs has been reported to increase the number of their catalytically active centers [23]. For example, Dehvari et al. incorporated Mn to carbon dots (MnNS: CDs) with higher photostability and antioxidant properties [24]. Das et al. incorporated nitrogen and sulphur in carbon dots with outstanding optical properties and specific binding sites for the precise identification of acetone [25]. Therefore, it's of great significance to develop of heteroatom-doped CDs with mimetic enzyme activities for cholesterol detection through dual-mode methods.

In this study, we successfully prepared the B, N-CDs via facile hydrothermal approach with 6-aminopyridine boronic acid as precursor. The B, N-CDs demonstrated intense blue luminescence and excellent peroxidase-like activity, which could catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to O2 •-, leading to the oxidation of colorless TMB into blue ox-TMB. Meanwhile, the B and N doping increased the active site of B, N-CDs, resulting in an augmentation of the fluorescence in the presence of H2O2, probably attributed to the generation of O2 •-. Thus, B, N-CDs could serve as an effective fluorometric and colorimetric sensor for the detection of H2O2. As the cholesterol could be oxidized to cholest-4-en-3-one and H2O2. Thus, the cholesterol levels could be indirectly measured using B, N-CDs. Subsequently, a dual-mode (fluorometric and colorimetric) detection method for H2O2 and cholesterol sensing was developed based on B, N-CDs. Finally, the practicality of B, N-CDs for cholesterol detection in human serum samples was validated through both fluorometric and colorimetric approaches. Therefore, this work provides a good platform for cholesterol-related clinical diagnosis and health management.

All chemicals were obtained from commercial sources and utilized without additional purification, except as otherwise noted. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was characterized through FEI Tecnai G2 F20. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained with Bruker D8 advance. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum were collected using a NICOLET6700 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) was performed using Thermo Scientific K-Alpha. UV-vis spectrum was obtained using a Shimadzu Model UV-1700 spectrometer. The fluorescence spectrums were recorded through a PerkinElmer LS 55 spectrometer.

Briefly, 19.1 mg of 6-aminopyridine boronic acid, 14.5 mg of citric acid, and 12.5 mg of iron chloride hexahydrate were added into 10 mL of N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF). Subsequently, the mixture was transformed to a Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to heating at 180 °C for 8 h. The synthesized solution underwent centrifugation to eliminate any solid precipitate followed by purification through dialysis for 48 h. Finally, the brownish-yellow solid-state B, N-CDs were obtained by freeze-drying (Scheme 1 ).

To investigate the peroxidase mimicking property of B, N-CDs, the catalytic oxidation of TMB with the addition of H2O2 was measured. Specifically, a certain amount of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 3.8) were added to (a) B, N-CDs and (b) B, N-CDs + TMB + H2O2, respectively, to bring the total volume of solution to 3 mL. After incubation for 15 min, the absorption spectrum of the resulting solution was monitored using the spectrophotometer. In addition, the peroxidase-like performance of B, N-CDs was assessed under varied incubation durations, temperatures, pH levels, and B, N-CDs concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady-state kinetic assays of B, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The steady-state kinetics of B, N-CDs with TMB and H2O2 as substrates were studied. Experiments was conducted using 100 μL B, N-CDs (1 mg mL−1) in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 3.8). Then, various concentration of H2O2 and TMB was added, and the absorbance changes were measured at 40 °C every 30 s. The values for
V m
and
K m
can be determined by fitting the substrate concentration against the reaction velocity using the Michaelis-Menten equation:
1 V
=
K m
V m
[ S ]
+
1
V m
where
V
is the initial rate of the reaction,
K m
is the Michaelis constant,
[ S ]
is the substrate concentration, and
V m
is the maximum velocity.

100 μL each of B, N-CDs (1 mg mL−1), TMB (5 mM), and varying concentrations of H2O2 were added into 2.7 mL of buffer (pH 3.8). UV-vis absorption of ox-TMB (652 nm) were monitored after incubation at 40 °C for 15 min. To detect cholesterol, 300 μL of PBS buffer (pH 7.4), 100 μL of cholesterol oxidase (20 U mL−1), and 100 μL cholesterol (0.5 % Triton X-100) at various concentrations were mixed and incubated for 30 min. Subsequently, the reaction mixture was incubated with 2.3 mL of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 3.8), along with 100 μL of B, N-CDs (1 mg mL−1), and 100 μL of TMB (5 mM), maintained at 40 °C for 15 min. The UV-vis spectrums were recorded and the absorbance variation (λab = 652 nm) was used to determine the cholesterol content.

100 μL of H2O2 at various concentrations and 500 μL of B, N-CDs (1 mg mL−1) were blended with 2.4 mL PBS buffer (pH 11). After incubation for 5 min, the PL spectrum (λex = 305 nm) was measured. To detect cholesterol, 100 μL of cholesterol oxidase (20 U mL−1), 100 μL cholesterol (0.5 % Triton X-100) at different concentrations, and 300 μL of PBS buffer (pH 7.4) were stored at room temperature for 30 min. The above solution was then incubated with 2.0 mL PBS buffer (pH 11) and 500 μL B, N-CDs (1 mg mL−1) at 40 °C for 15 min. The PL spectrum of the resulting solution was obtained and the cholesterol content was determined by the fluorescence growth factor F/F0 (F and F0 denote the emission intensity of B, N-CDs in the presence and absence of cholesterol).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of cholesterol in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate cholesterol levels in actual samples, serum from healthy volunteers was selected for analysis. The serum samples underwent an initial centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 30 min. Then, the supernatant was taken and filtered through a polyethersulfone membrane (PES) (0.22 μm). Finally, the filtrate was diluted by phosphate buffer solution (PBS) for further use. The standard addition method was carried out to estimate cholesterol level in serum samples [26].

The size and shape of B, N-CDs were identified by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM). In Fig. 1 A–B, B, N-CDs displayed the spherical shape with average diameter of 2.15 nm. The XRD pattern of B, N-CDs illustrated only one peak at 30° (Fig. 1C), corresponded to an amorphous structure of B, N-CDs [27]. The FT-IR spectrum was performed to verify the different functional groups of B, N-CDs. Fig. 1D showed the characteristic peaks at 3430 cm−1 (O–H and N–H), 2925 cm−1 (C–H), and 2854 cm−1 (–CH2–), 1623 cm−1 (CO), and 1396 cm−1 (–CC). This result indicated that B, N-CDs was functionalized with the abundant –OH, CO and –NH group.

Additionally, the XPS analysis was employed to examine the chemical composition of B, N-CDs. The full XPS result depicted the presence of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), along with minor quantities of iron (Fe) and boron (B) within B, N-CDs (Fig. 2 A). In the C 1s spectra (Fig. 2B), four bands at 284.2, 285.1, 285.8, and 287.4 eV were observed, attributed to C–C/CC, C–O/C–N, CN and CO, respectively [28]. The N 1s spectra (Fig. 2C) showed the presence of N–H (399.1 eV), N–C (399.3 eV), and NC (399.9 eV) bonds, respectively [29]. The O 1s spectra (Fig. 2D) displayed two peaks at 530.7 and 531.4 eV, indicating the presence of CO and C–O/O–B bonds, respectively [30]. In Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 2E), four peaks were identified at 709.8, 723.0, 713.8 and 726.9 eV respectively, where the first two peaks corresponded to Fe 2p3/2, while the last two corresponded to Fe 2p1/2, [31]. Three peaks at 190.7, 192.0 and 193.1 eV were observed in the B 1s spectrum (Fig. 2F), indicating the presence of BCO2, B2O3 and B–O groups, respectively [32]. These findings indicated the doping of B and Fe elements, along with the presence of functional groups (CO, –NH2 and –OH) on the surface of B, N-CDs.

The absorption spectra of B, N-CDs (Fig. 3 A) showed two peaks at 235 and 298 nm, attributed to π-π* transition of CC and n-π* transition of CO. The B, N-CDs emitted intense blue emission upon 365 nm UV irradiation. Upon 305 nm excitation, the strongest emission at 370 nm was obtained. In addition, the B, N-CDs displayed a typical excitation-independent behavior (Fig. 3B) and the fluorescence quantum yield of B, N-CDs was calculated to be 26.14 % with quinine sulfate as standard substance (Fig. 3C–D) [33].

The peroxidase-like performance of B, N-CDs was evaluated with TMB as substrate. As shown in Figs. S1A and B, N-CDs could accelerate the TMB oxidation, generating blue ox-TMB in the presence of H2O2 and leading to a characteristic absorption signal at 652 nm. However, in the B, N-CDs + TMB system, no obvious signal at 652 nm was observed, further evidencing the peroxidase-like property of B, N-CDs. Similar to horseradish peroxidase (HRP) [34], factors such as reaction time, temperature, pH value, and B, N-CDs concentration would affect the catalytic performance of B, N-CDs. At a pH of 3.8, B, N-CDs demonstrated the highest peroxidase-like activity (Fig. S1B). Additionally, B, N-CDs exhibited superior peroxidase-like activity when the temperature raised to 40 °C (Fig. S1C). Subsequently, the influence of reaction time on peroxidase-like activity of B, N-CDs was tested. As indicated in Fig. S1D, the absorbance of B, N-CDs + H2O2 + TMB system reached the maximum after incubation for 15 min. Finally, we investigated the effect of concentration on the activity of B, N-CDs. As displayed in Figs. S2 and B, N-CDs exhibited optimum activity when the concentration was set as 33.3 μg mL−1. Thus, pH of 3.8, temperature of 40 °C, 15 min of incubation, and concentration of 33.3 μg mL−1 were chosen as the optimal conditions for catalytic assay of B, N-CDs. In addition, B, N-CDs maintained stable peroxidase-like activity during 6 days of preservation at room temperature (Fig. S3), demonstrating their high stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady-state kinetic assays of B, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
In addition, the catalytic behavior of B, N-CDs was further explored by calculating the steady-state kinetic parameters. According to Michaelis-Menten model, the hyperbolic relationship between TMB/H2O2 concentration and reaction rate was observed, and the
K m
and
V m
values were derived from the corresponding Lineweaver-Burk curve (Fig. S4). As demonstrated in Table S1, the
K m
values of B, N-CDs were determined to be 0.022 and 0.286 mM toward H2O2 and TMB respectively. These values were found to be lower than reported nanozymes, implying a strong substrate affinity of B, N-CDs. Moreover, the
V m
values of B, N-CDs for H2O2 and TMB were valued as 20.10 × 10−8 and 8.77 × 10−8 M s−1, respectively, suggesting its high catalytic activity.

A significant relationship between the peroxidase mimicking activity of B, N-CDs and H2O2 concentration was observed when different concentration of H2O2 were added to the B, N-CDs + TMB system. Thus, utilizing the change in absorbance of ox-TMB around 652 nm, B, N-CDs showed promise as a sensor for detecting the concentrations of H2O2 and associated compounds. As indicated in Fig. S5, the absorbance at 652 nm exhibited linearity with the concentration of H2O2 ranging from 2 to 40 μM. The linear relationship could be formulated as A = 0.1522 [H2O2] + 0.0148 (R2 = 0.9957) and the detection limit (LOD) of H2O2 was calculated to be 0.023 μM.

In addition, the emission intensity of B, N-CDs gradually increased upon the addition of H2O2, which made it possible to detect cholesterol by fluorescence method. We then investigated the impact of H2O2 on the fluorescence of B, N-CDs under different incubation times and pH values. As displayed in Fig. S6A, after the addition of H2O2, the fluorescence of B, N-CDs remained essentially stable within the pH range of 4–10, but showed a stronger fluorescence response at pH 11. The pH effects on fluorescence of B, N-CDs was probably attributable to changes in surface state. Moreover, the fluorescence response of B, N-CDs increased with the prolonged incubation time, peaking after 5 min of incubation (Fig. S6B). Consequently, the subsequent fluorometric assays were conducted at pH 11 after incubation for 5 min.

As indicated in Fig. 5 A–B, the fluorescence signal of B, N-CDs gradually increased with the cholesterol concentration ranging from 0 to 500 μM. The variation in fluorescence intensity (F/F0) of B, N-CDs + ChOx system was linearly correlated with the cholesterol content in the wide range of 15–160 μM (Fig. 5C), with a linear correlation of F/F0 = 0.00177C + 1.0716 (R2 = 0.9990). Based on this equation, the LOD was calculated to be 2.31 μM, which is significantly lower than previous nanomaterials (Table 1). Additionally, the selectivity of fluorescence response of B, N-CDs toward cholesterol was illustrated in Fig. 5D. When different interfering substances including glucose, Na+, K+, Mg2+, ascorbic acid, uric acid, Hcy, Phe, GSH, Val, Gly, Ile and Lys was added, only cholesterol caused a remarkable augmentation in fluorescence, even when the concentration of interfering substances was 15 times higher than that of cholesterol, demonstrating the high selectivity of fluorometric assay for cholesterol detection. The fluorometric method of B, N-CDs for cholesterol detection exhibited a wider or equivalent range of selectivity compared to other reported materials (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of cholesterol detection by B, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism for cholesterol detection using B, N-CDs was illustrated in Fig. 6 A. The specific mechanism was verified by employing p-benzoquinone, D-histidine and isopropanol as scavengers, which specifically capture O2 •-, 1O2 and •OH, respectively. As displayed in Fig. 6B, the catalytic activity of B, N-CDs remained unaffected by D-histidine or isopropanol. Reversely, with the introduction of p-benzoquinone, there was a noticeable decline in the absorbance of the “B, N-CDs + TMB + H2O2” solution, suggesting that the radical responsible for TMB oxidition was likely O2 •-. Moreover, the fluorescence enhancement of B, N-CDs upon H2O2 was investigated similarly. As shown in Fig. 6C, the introduction of p-benzoquinone noticeably inhibited the emission enhancement of B, N-CDs, suggesting the O2 •- was the primary radical that enhanced the fluorescence. Meanwhile, the heteroatom in B, N-CDs increased the active sites on the surface, which would improve the production efficiency of O2 •- radical [39], resulting in the enhancement of fluorescence intensity. Finally, the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was conducted to further demonstrate the generation of O2 •- in the system. As indicated in Fig. 6D, the “B, N-CDs + H2O2 + DMPO” system revealed the distinctive peak of DMPO/O2 •- adduct, indicating the production of O2 •-.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of cholesterol in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the practical feasibility of B, N-CDs for colorimetric and fluorometric detection, the standard addition approach was used to assess the cholesterol levels in serum samples. Firstly, the diluted serum samples were supplemented with a range of cholesterol concentrations (10, 25, and 50 μM). The cholesterol content was then measured by colorimetric and fluorometric methods based on B, N-CDs. As presented in Table 2 , the cholesterol concentrations in serum that determined by proposed methods were almost consistent with the spiked concentration, indicating the high accuracy of the colorimetric and fluorometric methods. Moreover, the obtained recovery from both methods was ranged from 95.34 to 100.20 %, with RSDs less than 4.55 %, suggesting the high sensitivity for the determination of cholesterol. These results demonstrated that the dual-mode method based on B, N-CDs has extraordinary potential for cholesterol detection in real application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of the cytotoxicity of B, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The biocompatibility of B, N-CDs was tested using CCK-8 assay. Specifically, the viability of Hepa1-6 cells was measured after exposure to various concentrations of B, N-CDs. As depicted in Fig. S7, the cancer cells retained highly viability even at 200 μg mL−1 of B, N-CDs, indicating their minimal cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility.

In summary, a blue-emissive B, N co-doped carbon dots (B, N-CDs) was synthesized by hydrothermal method. B, N-CDs exhibited remarkable peroxidase-like performance, efficiently catalyzing the oxidation of TMB into blue ox-TMB upon the addition of H2O2. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of B, N-CDs gradually enhanced by adding H2O2. Thus, a peroxidase-like activity regulation strategy of B, N-CDs system was devised for colorimetric and fluorometric detection of H2O2. Since cholesterol was readily oxidized to form H2O2 in the presence of ChOx, an optical dual-mode sensing platform based on B, N-CDs was established for cholesterol determination. The high selectivity of B, N-CDs towards cholesterol detection and the proposed mechanism were validated through interference experiments and free radical testing method, respectively. Finally, the B, N-CDs were effectively utilized for the accurate and sensitive determination of cholesterol levels in actual samples. Thus, this research developed a simple method for fabricating B, N co-doped carbon dots with excellent peroxidase mimicking activity and intense fluorescence, thereby acting as promising nanozymes for cholesterol detection through both colorimetric and fluorometric methods.

Yuyan Zhu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Ruilin Zhang: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhiyuan Hu: Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Fengshou Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (grant no. 21601142) and the Innovation Project of Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry (grant no. 2024BEEA06).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2024.126471.

Small Methods
